description: Bizon Tech Internal Knowledge Base - Custom commands and diagnostics

rtx_6000_pro_fan_curve.title: RTX 6000 Pro / RTX PRO 6000 GPU Fan Curve Setup
rtx_6000_pro_fan_curve.description: IMPORTANT: This ONLY applies to RTX 6000 Pro and RTX PRO 6000 GPUs. Do NOT use for other GPU models.

workflow.trigger: When user reports RTX 6000 Pro or RTX PRO 6000 getting too hot, or asks to set up fan curve for these specific GPUs
steps: 1. First, ASK the user: 'Does your RTX 6000 Pro have a blower-style cooler (single fan) or a dual-fan cooler?'
2. Wait for user response before running any commands
3. BEFORE running Docker containers, install nvidia-container-toolkit if not already installed
4. If BLOWER/SINGLE FAN: Run the v1.0 Docker commands
5. If DUAL FAN: Run the v2.0 Docker commands

commands.name: Install NVIDIA Container Toolkit (REQUIRED FIRST)
commands.command: sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y nvidia-container-toolkit && sudo systemctl restart docker
commands.description: MUST run this first to enable GPU access in Docker containers. Installs nvidia-container-toolkit and restarts Docker.

commands.name: Pull fan control image (Blower/Single-Fan - v1.0)
commands.description: Downloads the fan control Docker image for single-fan/blower GPUs like A6000

commands.name: Run fan control (Blower/Single-Fan - v1.0)
commands.description: Starts the fan control container for single-fan/blower GPUs

commands.name: Pull fan control image (Dual-Fan - v2.0)
commands.description: Downloads the fan control Docker image for dual-fan GPUs like RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell

commands.name: Run fan control (Dual-Fan - v2.0)
commands.description: Starts the fan control container for dual-fan GPUs

commands.name: Check if fan control container is running
commands.description: Verifies the fan control container is active

commands.name: Check fan control logs
commands.command: docker logs $(docker ps -q --filter ancestor=gimel12/gpu-fan-control:v1.0) 2>/dev/null || docker logs $(docker ps -q --filter ancestor=gimel12/gpu-fan-control:v2.0) 2>/dev/null || echo 'No fan control container running'
commands.description: Shows logs from the fan control container

commands.name: Verify fan curve is working
commands.description: Check GPU fan speeds and temperatures - fans should be ~40% at idle, 100% when temp >75°C

verification.description: To verify the fan curve is working correctly for RTX 6000 Pro:
expected_behavior: At IDLE (low temps): Fan speed should be around 40%
At HIGH LOAD (temps >75°C): Fan speed should ramp up to 100%
Run nvidia-smi to check current fan speeds and temperatures

rtx_6000_pro_fan_curve.notes: This fan curve solution is EXCLUSIVELY for RTX 6000 Pro and RTX PRO 6000 GPUs. Do NOT suggest this for other GPU models like RTX 4090, RTX 3090, etc. Always ask the user about their fan configuration (blower vs dual-fan) BEFORE running any Docker commands.

gpu_diagnostics.title: GPU Diagnostic Commands

commands.name: Check GPU Xid errors
commands.description: Shows NVIDIA Xid errors which indicate GPU hardware issues

commands.name: GPU memory test
commands.description: Detailed GPU memory information including ECC errors

commands.name: GPU power and clocks
commands.description: Shows current clock speeds and power draw

commands.name: Reset GPU
commands.description: Resets the GPU - use with caution

commands.name: Enable persistence mode
commands.description: Enables persistence mode to keep driver loaded

gpu_diagnostics.notes: Xid 79 = GPU fallen off bus (hardware issue). Xid 31/32 = memory errors. Xid 43/45 = power issues.

ecc_memory.title: ECC Memory Diagnostics

commands.name: Check ECC errors
commands.description: Shows corrected and uncorrected ECC memory errors

commands.name: ECC summary
commands.description: Summary of memory controller errors

commands.name: EDAC status
commands.description: Lists DIMM labels from EDAC subsystem

commands.name: Memory errors in dmesg
commands.description: Memory-related errors from kernel messages

ecc_memory.notes: Corrected errors (CE) are self-healing. Uncorrected errors (UE) indicate failing memory that needs replacement.

stress_testing.title: Stress Testing Commands

commands.name: Check if stress-ng is running
commands.description: Shows if CPU stress test is active

commands.name: Check if gpu-burn is running
commands.description: Shows if GPU burn test is active

commands.name: Start CPU stress test (8 hours)
commands.description: Starts 8-hour CPU stress test in background

commands.name: Stop CPU stress test
commands.description: Kills any running stress-ng processes

commands.name: Check stress test log
commands.description: Shows recent stress test output

stress_testing.notes: Always monitor temperatures during stress tests. Stop immediately if temps exceed safe limits.

gpu_burn_test.title: GPU Burn Stress Test
gpu_burn_test.description: GPU burn test is located at /home/bizon/Downloads/gpu-burn. Use this for GPU stress testing.

workflow.trigger: When user asks to run GPU test, GPU burn, GPU stress test, or test GPUs
steps: 1. Navigate to /home/bizon/Downloads/gpu-burn
2. Run ./gpu_burn with -tc flag and duration in SECONDS
3. Duration conversion: 1 hour = 3600 seconds, 2 hours = 7200 seconds, 8 hours = 28800 seconds

commands.name: Check if gpu-burn exists
commands.description: Verify gpu_burn binary exists

commands.name: Check if GPU burn is already running
commands.command: pgrep -a gpu_burn || echo 'GPU burn not running'
commands.description: Check for existing gpu_burn processes

commands.name: Run GPU burn for 1 hour
commands.description: Start 1-hour GPU burn test (3600 seconds) in background

commands.name: Run GPU burn for 2 hours
commands.description: Start 2-hour GPU burn test (7200 seconds) in background

commands.name: Run GPU burn for 8 hours
commands.description: Start 8-hour GPU burn test (28800 seconds) in background

commands.name: Stop GPU burn test
commands.description: Kills any running gpu_burn processes

commands.name: Check GPU burn log
commands.description: Shows recent GPU burn test output

commands.name: Monitor GPUs during burn test
commands.description: Check GPU temps and utilization during test

gpu_burn_test.notes: IMPORTANT: gpu_burn is located at /home/bizon/Downloads/gpu-burn/gpu_burn. The -tc flag enables tensor core testing. Duration is in SECONDS not hours. Always monitor GPU temperatures during the test.

bios_info.title: BIOS and System Information

commands.name: BIOS version
commands.description: Shows BIOS manufacturer and version

commands.name: System info
commands.description: Shows system manufacturer, model, and serial number

commands.name: Memory slots
commands.description: Shows installed memory modules

commands.name: CPU info from DMI
commands.description: CPU details from system DMI

bios_info.notes: dmidecode requires sudo for accurate information

gpu_tdp_wattage.title: GPU TDP / Wattage / Power Limit Change
gpu_tdp_wattage.description: Change the GPU power limit (TDP/wattage) on the system by modifying the script at /home/bizon/.gpu/gpu_tdp.sh. CRITICAL: You MUST modify the script file FIRST, then run it. Do NOT run nvidia-smi -pl directly — always go through the script file.

workflow.trigger: When user asks to change GPU wattage, TDP, power limit, or power draw
steps: STEP 1: If the user did NOT specify the wattage, ASK: 'What wattage (power limit) would you like to set for the GPUs? (e.g. 100, 300, 350, 500)' — WAIT for their answer before doing anything.

STEP 2: Once you have the wattage number, MODIFY THE SCRIPT FILE FIRST. The file is /home/bizon/.gpu/gpu_tdp.sh and it is owned by root, so you MUST use sudo to write to it. Run this command (replacing WATTAGE with the actual number): sudo bash -c "cat > /home/bizon/.gpu/gpu_tdp.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
echo 'root' | sudo -S nvidia-smi -pm 1
echo 'root' | sudo -S nvidia-smi -pl WATTAGE
EOF"
STEP 3: VERIFY the file was written correctly by reading it back: cat /home/bizon/.gpu/gpu_tdp.sh — confirm the power limit line is UNCOMMENTED and shows the correct wattage number.
STEP 4: Only AFTER confirming the file is correct, run the script to apply: sudo bash /home/bizon/.gpu/gpu_tdp.sh
STEP 5: Verify the GPUs changed: nvidia-smi --query-gpu=index,name,power.limit,power.draw --format=csv
STEP 6: Report to the user: show the new power limits AND confirm the script file was updated.

commands.name: Step 1 - Check current GPU power limits
commands.description: Shows current power limit, default limit, and actual power draw for each GPU

commands.name: Step 2 - MODIFY the script file (replace WATTAGE with the number, e.g. 100, 300, 500)
commands.command:
sudo bash -c "cat > /home/bizon/.gpu/gpu_tdp.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
echo 'root' | sudo -S nvidia-smi -pm 1
echo 'root' | sudo -S nvidia-smi -pl WATTAGE
EOF"
commands.description: CRITICAL: This MUST be done FIRST before applying. Writes the gpu_tdp.sh script with the power limit UNCOMMENTED and set to the requested wattage. Uses sudo because the file is owned by root. Replace WATTAGE with the actual number.

commands.name: Step 3 - VERIFY the file was modified correctly
commands.description: Read the file back to confirm it was written correctly. The power limit line must be UNCOMMENTED and show the correct wattage.

commands.name: Step 4 - Apply by running the script
commands.description: Runs the gpu_tdp.sh script to apply the power limit. Only run AFTER confirming the file was modified correctly in Step 3.

commands.name: Step 5 - Verify GPU power limits changed
commands.description: Verify that the new power limit was applied successfully to all GPUs

commands.name: Reset GPU to default wattage (only if user asks to reset)
commands.command:
sudo bash -c "cat > /home/bizon/.gpu/gpu_tdp.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
echo 'root' | sudo -S nvidia-smi -pm 1
#echo 'root' | sudo -S nvidia-smi -pl 300
EOF"
commands.description: Resets the script so GPUs run at default wattage. The power limit line is COMMENTED OUT with # so it won't execute.

gpu_tdp_wattage.notes: CRITICAL RULES: 1) NEVER run nvidia-smi -pl directly — ALWAYS modify the script file first, then run the script. 2) The file /home/bizon/.gpu/gpu_tdp.sh is owned by root, so you MUST use 'sudo' to write to it. 3) ALWAYS verify the file contents after writing by running 'cat /home/bizon/.gpu/gpu_tdp.sh' before applying. 4) The default state has the power limit line COMMENTED OUT with # — to change wattage you must UNCOMMENT it (remove the #) and set the number. 5) Follow the steps IN ORDER: modify file → verify file → run script → verify GPUs.

storage_health.title: Storage Health Commands

commands.name: List all NVMe drives
commands.description: Lists all NVMe device nodes

commands.name: NVMe health (first drive)
commands.description: Shows SMART health for first NVMe drive

commands.name: Check all drives health
commands.command: for d in /dev/sd? /dev/nvme?n1; do echo "=== $d ==="; sudo smartctl -H $d 2>/dev/null; done
commands.description: Quick health check on all drives

commands.name: Drive temperatures
commands.command: sudo smartctl -A /dev/nvme0 2>/dev/null | grep -i temp || sudo nvme smart-log /dev/nvme0 | grep -i temp
commands.description: Shows drive temperature

storage_health.notes: NVMe drives use nvme-cli or smartctl. SATA drives use smartctl only.

rtx_6000_pro_overheating.symptom: RTX 6000 Pro or RTX PRO 6000 GPU running hot / overheating
steps: 1. ASK the user: Does your RTX 6000 Pro have a blower-style cooler (single fan) or a dual-fan cooler?
2. For BLOWER/SINGLE FAN GPUs: Run 'sudo docker pull gimel12/gpu-fan-control:v1.0' then 'docker run -d --restart unless-stopped --privileged --gpus all gimel12/gpu-fan-control:v1.0'
3. For DUAL FAN GPUs: Run 'sudo docker pull gimel12/gpu-fan-control:v2.0' then 'docker run -d --restart unless-stopped --privileged --gpus all gimel12/gpu-fan-control:v2.0'
4. Verify with: nvidia-smi --query-gpu=index,name,fan.speed,temperature.gpu --format=csv
5. Expected: ~40% fan at idle, 100% fan when temp >75°C
rtx_6000_pro_overheating.important: This solution is ONLY for RTX 6000 Pro and RTX PRO 6000 GPUs. Do NOT use for other GPU models.

steps: Check lspci | grep -i nvidia
Check dmesg | grep -i nvidia
Verify GPU is seated properly
Check PCIe power connectors
Try nvidia-smi -r to reset

high_temperatures.symptom: CPU or GPU running hot (general - not RTX 6000 Pro specific)
steps: Check fan speeds with ipmitool sensor | grep fan
Verify airflow and dust buildup
Check thermal paste application
Ensure proper case ventilation
For RTX 6000 Pro specifically, see rtx_6000_pro_overheating troubleshooting

gpu_wattage_change.symptom: User wants to change GPU wattage, TDP, or power limit
steps: 1. Ask user what wattage they want if not specified — WAIT for answer

2. MODIFY THE FILE FIRST using sudo: sudo bash -c "cat > /home/bizon/.gpu/gpu_tdp.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
echo 'root' | sudo -S nvidia-smi -pm 1
echo 'root' | sudo -S nvidia-smi -pl WATTAGE
EOF" (replace WATTAGE with the number)
3. VERIFY the file was written: cat /home/bizon/.gpu/gpu_tdp.sh (confirm the line is uncommented with correct wattage)
4. THEN run the script: sudo bash /home/bizon/.gpu/gpu_tdp.sh
5. Verify GPUs: nvidia-smi --query-gpu=index,name,power.limit,power.draw --format=csv
6. Report results to user — confirm BOTH the file and GPUs are updated
gpu_wattage_change.important: NEVER run nvidia-smi -pl directly. ALWAYS modify the script file first with sudo (file is owned by root), verify it, then run the script.

steps: Run sudo ras-mc-ctl --errors to identify affected DIMM
Check sudo ras-mc-ctl --summary for error counts
If correctable errors only, monitor for increase
If uncorrectable errors, replace affected DIMM
